nativeRange.habitat: Still or slow-moving shallow water in lowland rainforest — densely planted pools, swamps, and the margins of slow rivers, generally with soft substrate and subdued lighting.
nativeRange.biotope: Equatorial African blackwater / tannin-stained shallows; leaf litter, soft mud, and submerged vegetation.

decorPreferences: soft sand or smooth fine gravel
tightly fitting lid with surface access for air-breathing

diet.notes: Generally advised to feed sinking carnivore foods — frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, blackworms, tubifex, and sinking pellets formulated for frogs or carnivorous fish. Adults are commonly fed every other day; juveniles daily. They are slow, near-sighted feeders, so target-feeding helps ensure each frog eats. They have been known to swallow anything that fits in the mouth, so avoid pairing with very small tankmates or shrimp fry.

goodWith: small peaceful community fish (e.g. Corydoras, small rasboras, ember tetras)
avoidWith: Xenopus laevis (African Clawed Frog) — much larger predator
very small fish such as neon-tetra-size species that fit in the frog's mouth
fast competitive feeders that out-compete the frogs

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by James Gathany (CDC), 4000x2804. Striking, tack-sharp whole-body shot of a live frog climbing a green plant stem against vivid red gravel; clearly shows the diagnostic fully-webbed front feet, side-set eyes, pointed snout and speckled skin.
Photo by Stuart Halliday, 2759x1664. Sharp full-body lateral view of a live frog in a planted sand aquarium, showing webbed hind feet and pointed snout; clean single-subject reference angle.
1000x504. Two live frogs in amplexus on gravel; sharp, natural speckled coloration, illustrates the social/paired behavior of this shoaling species (modest resolution).

summary: The African Dwarf Frog (Hymenochirus boettgeri) is a small, fully aquatic frog from Central and West Africa, widely kept as a beginner-friendly community animal. It reaches 3–6.5 cm, thrives in groups of three or more, and needs surface access for air-breathing in a warm, gently filtered tank.
careNotes: African Dwarf Frogs are fully aquatic but breathe air — they have lungs and must reach the surface every few minutes, so a low water column with easy surface access and a tightly-fitting lid (they have been known to climb out if given the chance) is generally advised. They are tropical animals: a stable 22–26°C is commonly recommended, and unheated rooms can stress them. Their permeable amphibian skin makes them sensitive to chemical contaminants; copper-based fish medications, chlorine/chloramine, and most snail/parasite treatments should be avoided or used with great caution. Susceptibility to the chytrid fungus (Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis) is moderate, so quarantining new arrivals and not mixing with wild-collected amphibians is generally advised. Substrate is best either bare-bottom or fine smooth sand — large or sharp gravel has been reported to cause impaction or oral injuries during feeding. They are very near-sighted and slow eaters, so target feeding with a turkey baster or pipette is commonly recommended in community tanks so they aren't out-competed. They have been known to swallow anything that fits in the mouth, including dwarf shrimp and neon-tetra-size fish, so tankmates should be selected accordingly. CRITICAL: African Dwarf Frogs are very often mis-sold or confused with the African Clawed Frog (Xenopus laevis), which grows roughly 4–5× larger and is a voracious predator that will eat tankmates and outgrow most aquariums. ADFs have fully webbed front feet, side-set eyes, and a pointed snout; Xenopus have unwebbed front 'fingers', dorsally-set eyes, and a flat snout. The trade names 'African Dwarf Frog' and 'Hymenochirus curtipes' (a sister species) are used interchangeably in aquarium retail — H. boettgeri and H. curtipes are visually and behaviorally indistinguishable in the hobby, and both names refer to the same animal as sold.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in home aquariums with moderate effort. A drop in water level followed by a rise in temperature toward 26–28°C and a large water change generally triggers spawning. Males call with a soft buzzing hum and clasp females in inguinal amplexus, usually at night. Females are reported to release 150–700 buoyant eggs that float at the surface; eggs typically hatch in 1–2 days at >22°C. Adults readily eat their own eggs and tadpoles, so removing eggs to a separate rearing container is generally advised. Tadpoles are filter-feeding microcarnivores (infusoria, microworms, baby brine shrimp) and metamorphose in roughly 4–8 weeks.

primary.name: Josh's Frogs
primary.notes: Primary URL was unreachable (redirect loop) on 2026-06-12; the canonical care-sheet URL (joshsfrogs.com/care-sheet/african-dwarf-frogs-how-to-care-for-the-hymenochiris-genus) also returned 403. Cited as the assigned primary; husbandry values were sourced from secondaries and cross-verified across four independent sources.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Confirmed peaceful temperament, tropical 24–27°C (75–80°F), pH 6.8–7.8, 10 gal minimum, omnivore/micropredator, will climb out without a lid. Lists scientificName as H. curtipes — consistent with the H. boettgeri / H. curtipes trade-name consolidation policy.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (AquariumBreeder)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size 5–7 cm, lifespan 5–8 yr, temperature 23–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 5–20 kH 4–12, 5 gal floor, chytrid susceptibility, skin permeability, sand-or-bare substrate, breeding 150–700 eggs.

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Cross-checked size up to 7.5 cm, temp 20–25°C, pH 5–7, 5 gal/frog, social group of 3+, sand substrate, distinguishes from Xenopus.

additional.name: Reptile.guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked size up to ~7.5 cm, lifespan ~5 yr, temp 22–25.5°C, pH 5–7.8, gH 5–20 kH 4–15, breeding behavior, Xenopus distinction.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Hymenochirus boettgeri)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback: family Pipidae, order Anura; native range Nigeria, Cameroon, Equatorial Guinea, Gabon, CAR, DRC; lowland rainforest pools and slow rivers; originally classified as Xenopus boettgeri, reflecting historical genus confusion.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Triggered by simulated rainy-season cues (cool water change followed by a temperature rise toward 26–28°C). Females release 150–700 floating eggs; adults eat their own eggs and tadpoles, so eggs are generally moved to a separate rearing container. Tadpoles are microcarnivorous filter feeders that metamorphose in roughly 4–8 weeks.
